statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, build a min-heap from it and then extract elements one by one to get the sorted array in descending order.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The min-heap built from the array is [2, 3, 8, 5, 9]. Extracting elements one by one gives the sorted array in descending order.
The min-heap built from the array is [1, 2, 3]. Extracting elements one by one gives the sorted array in descending order.

hints.text: To solve this problem, you need to first understand what a min-heap is and how it's constructed from an array of integers.

The process of building a min-heap involves ensuring that for every node i, the value of the node is less than or equal to the values of its children. You should start from the last non-leaf node and perform the heapify operation.

Once you have a min-heap, you can extract elements one by one to get the sorted array in descending order. This involves removing the minimum element (root of the min-heap), placing it at the end of the array, and then heapifying the reduced heap.

editorial.title: Heaps Theory: Unlocking Efficient Sorting
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved efficiently by leveraging the properties of a heap data structure. A heap is a complete binary tree where each parent node is less than or equal to its children. This property allows for efficient sorting by repeatedly 'removing' the smallest element (the root) and re-heapifying the remaining elements.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve sorting the array using a simple algorithm like bubble sort or insertion sort, which would result in a time complexity of O(N^2). This is inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to use heap sort, which has a time complexity of O(N log N) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.optimalSolution: The heap sort algorithm works by first building a min-heap from the input array. Then, it repeatedly swaps the root node (smallest element) with the last node in the heap, reduces the heap size, and calls heapify on the root node to maintain the heap property.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of heap sort can be proven by showing that the heap property is maintained throughout the algorithm. The heapify function ensures that the subtree rooted at a given node is a valid min-heap. By repeatedly applying heapify, the algorithm ensures that the entire array is sorted in ascending order.
editorial.dryRun: For an array of size N, the heap sort algorithm first builds a min-heap in O(N) time. Then, it performs N-1 swaps and heapify operations, each taking O(log N) time. This results in a total time complexity of O(N log N).
commonMistakes: Not properly maintaining the heap property during the sorting process
Failing to handle edge cases, such as an empty or single-element array
Using an incorrect data structure, such as a max-heap instead of a min-heap
Not optimizing the heapify function for performance
Incorrectly implementing the swap operation during the sorting process
Not considering the space complexity of the algorithm
